title: How to work a treble
subtitle: The medium-height stitch that builds granny squares, blankets, and most amigurumi at scale.
excerpt: A treble is one yarn-over before inserting the hook, then three loops worked off two at a time. It sits about 2 cm tall on a 4 mm hook in DK yarn and is the workhorse of most crochet patterns.
sourceNotes:
Method drawn from two public-domain references:

• Weldon's Practical Crochet (first series, 1880s), entry for the long stitch (Victorian name for what is now called the treble in UK terminology). Internet Archive.
• Caulfeild & Saward, The Dictionary of Needlework (1882), entry for Crochet, sub-entry on the four basic stitches. Internet Archive.

UK terminology is the canonical naming on this entry per the site convention; the US equivalent (double crochet) is given in brackets at each mention.

glossaryTerms.definition: Wrapping the working yarn over the hook from back to front, creating an extra loop on the hook before the next pull-through. Abbreviated yo.

content.text: A treble (UK terminology; US: double crochet) is a medium-tall stitch made by wrapping the yarn over the hook once before inserting it, then working three loops off the hook two at a time. On a 4 mm hook in DK yarn each treble sits about 2 cm tall.

The treble is the most-used stitch in granny squares, blankets, and most beginner patterns because it builds fabric quickly without leaving the gaps a double treble does. Almost every crochet pattern uses it.
UK and US naming

Crochet has two parallel naming systems. The UK "treble" and the US "double crochet" describe the same stitch. The UK "double crochet" describes a shorter stitch which the US calls "single crochet". Patterns written in UK terminology can be a row taller than the same pattern written in US terminology by the same author, so the convention matters. This entry uses UK throughout; the US equivalent is given in brackets at each step.
What you need
A 4 mm crochet hook and a ball of DK yarn in any smooth, light colour. A starting chain of about 20 stitches is plenty for a practice row.
Working a single treble
Start with a chain. Skip the first three chains (this counts as the first treble of the row). Then for each subsequent treble:
Make a
yarn-over
(yo). You now have two loops on the hook: the working loop and the yarn-over.
Insert the hook into the next chain (or stitch on later rows). Yarn-over again. Pull this new yarn-over back through the chain. Three loops on the hook.
Yarn-over. Pull through the first two loops on the hook. Two loops left on the hook.
Yarn-over. Pull through the remaining two loops. One loop left on the hook.
One treble made. The next treble is worked the same way into the next chain. At the end of the row, turn the work, make three chains as the first treble of the new row, and work back across.
The pattern of the stitch
The basic crochet stitch family is one motion, repeated and varied:
Double crochet (UK; US: single crochet): no starting yarn-over. Insert hook, yarn-over, pull through (2 loops on hook), yarn-over, pull through both. Short and dense.
Half treble (UK; US: half double crochet): one starting yarn-over, then pull through all three loops at once. Medium height.
Treble (UK; US: double crochet): one starting yarn-over, three pull-throughs (two at a time). The workhorse covered in this entry.
Double treble (UK; US: treble crochet): two starting yarn-overs, four pull-throughs. Tall and open.
Once the treble feels automatic, the others are the same motion with a different number of yarn-overs at the start.
Common mistakes

attrs.heading: What goes wrong on a first treble row
attrs.intro: Four things every new crocheter does at some point.

items.symptom: The row gets shorter as you go (loses stitches)
items.cause: The last stitch of the row was worked into the same chain as the previous stitch, or the turning chain wasn't worked into on the return row.
items.fix: Count the stitches at the end of every row and compare to the count at the end of row 1. The turning chain (3 chains at the start of each new row) counts as the first treble; the last treble of every row goes into the top of the previous row's turning chain.

items.symptom: The row gets longer as you go (gains stitches)
items.cause: An extra treble was worked into the turning chain at the start of the row, or two trebles were worked into the same chain.
items.fix: Use a stitch marker on the first treble of each row. At the end of the row, count back to it and confirm the count matches.

items.symptom: The trebles look stretched and uneven
items.cause: Yarn tension was too loose, or each pull-through was made with the loops far apart.
items.fix: Pull each yarn-over down to the level of the hook's neck before the next pull-through. The loops should sit close together on the hook, not spread along the shaft.

items.symptom: The hook keeps slipping out of the loop
items.cause: The working loop is too loose, or the hook is the wrong direction for the working yarn.
items.fix: Tighten the working loop after each stitch so it grips the hook just below the head. The flat-faced hook (not the inline shape) should sit so the hook opening faces you when working.

content.text: Where this goes next

A row of trebles is enough to make a dishcloth, a scarf, or a granny square. From there, working trebles in groups of three (with chains between) is what builds the classic granny square; working trebles back and forth on a foundation chain is the start of every striped blanket. The motion stays the same; the arrangement changes.
